legal_or_institutional_claim: Chapter 617 broadly prohibits public-sector collective bargaining and union recognition for all Texas public employees, safety and non-safety alike; a violating contract is void; striking employees forfeit civil-service/reemployment rights
source_title_or_description: Texas Government Code Chapter 617, Section 617.002 (Collective Bargaining by Public Employees Prohibited)
notes: Corroborated by law.justia.com's aggregation of the same official statute text; this is the default rule Chapters 174/142/146 carve out from

legal_or_institutional_claim: Chapter 174 (Fire and Police Employee Relations Act) grants fire fighters and/or police officers full collective-bargaining rights, but only in a political subdivision that adopts the chapter by local referendum election
source_title_or_description: Texas Local Government Code Section 174.051 (Adoption Election) and Section 174.003 (Definitions)
notes: Adoption petition threshold: lesser of 20,000 signatures or 5% of qualified voters in the preceding general election; a repeal election is separately available under Section 174.053

legal_or_institutional_claim: Chapter 174 does not require compulsory arbitration; even where a party requests arbitration, both sides must separately agree in writing to arbitrate within 5 days
source_title_or_description: Texas Local Government Code Section 174.163 (Compulsory Arbitration Not Required) and Section 174.153 (Request for Arbitration; Agreement to Arbitrate)
notes: RESOLVED 2026-07-08: Section 174.153(a)'s cross-reference to Section 174.1535 ('Mandatory Arbitration') is now confirmed to be a narrow exception, not a general one -- see the new row below. This general no-compulsory-arbitration rule remains accurate for every Texas bargaining unit outside that narrow exception

legal_or_institutional_claim: Section 174.1535 creates a true compulsory-arbitration exception to Section 174.163, but ONLY for a fire department serving a municipality with population >=1.9 million; binding arbitration is mandatory if the parties cannot settle within 61 days after the governing body rejects a bargained contract
source_title_or_description: Texas Local Government Code Section 174.1535 (Mandatory Arbitration)
notes: RESOLVED 2026-07-08 (see texas_ohio_legal_followup_source_audit_2026-07-08.md Sec.2): directly fetched and read this session, corroborated by an independent secondary summary (a 2023 Texas Senate bill-analysis document, capitol.texas.gov/tlodocs/88R/analysis/html/SB00736I.htm, describing the same population threshold and mechanics). Houston (~2.39 million) is the only Texas municipality meeting the 1.9-million threshold; no other candidate city (San Antonio, Dallas, Austin, Fort Worth) is close. This makes Sec.174.1535 a Houston-fire-only compulsory-arbitration provision, not a general Chapter 174 feature

legal_or_institutional_claim: Houston Fire Fighters (HPFFA, IAFF Local 341) operate under full Chapter 174 collective bargaining (voter-adopted 2003), not Chapter 142 meet-and-confer as previously assumed; Houston Police (HPOU) remain under Chapter 142 meet-and-confer
source_title_or_description: Houston Professional Fire Fighters Association (IAFF Local 341), 'Our History' page; corroborated by a 2024 Houston City Council committee settlement document and IAFF's own national reporting on a related court ruling
notes: RESOLVED 2026-07-08 (see texas_ohio_legal_followup_source_audit_2026-07-08.md Sec.4): corrects the prior session's secondary-sourced classification of Houston as a uniform Ch. 142 meet-and-confer city for both police and fire. The union's own historical account is a union-source, not a primary statute or an official city record of the 2003 adoption election itself -- the election's official record was not independently located this session

legal_or_institutional_claim: Chapter 142/146 'meet and confer' is a lighter-weight, also locally adopted (by ordinance, not necessarily a full referendum) process; the statute does not require either party to reach agreement on any issue
source_title_or_description: Texas Local Government Code Chapter 142, Subchapter on Recognition of Police Officers'/Firefighters' Associations
notes: Confirmed via a web-search synthesis of the official statute's structure and quoted language; the full statutory text was not directly fetched and read section-by-section this session

legal_or_institutional_claim: Chapter 146 is NOT a general local-option chapter any city can choose to adopt -- Section 146.001(a) restricts it to 'a municipality with a population of 1.5 million or more,' with no election or ordinance-adoption step; it authorizes meet-and-confer for non-safety 'covered employees' (municipal employees other than department heads or Ch. 143/174-covered police/fire), with no arbitration/impasse mechanism found in the chapter's own text (Secs. 146.001-146.017)
source_title_or_description: Texas Local Government Code Section 146.001 (Applicability) and Section 146.002 (Definitions)
notes: RESOLVED 2026-07-08 (see texas_ohio_legal_followup_source_audit_2026-07-08.md Sec.3): directly fetched and read this session, correcting the prior session's 'municipalities that choose to adopt it' framing. Houston (~2.39 million) is the only Texas municipality meeting the 1.5-million threshold; no other candidate city qualifies. This closes the prior session's flagged follow-up

legal_or_institutional_claim: HOPE (Houston Organization of Public Employees), AFSCME Local 123, has been Houston's Chapter 146 non-safety meet-and-confer bargaining agent since 2008, reportedly representing 12,000+ municipal employees across most city departments, with successor agreements in 2015, 2018, 2021, and 2024
source_title_or_description: HOPE AFSCME Local 123 website and resources page; corroborated by AFSCME's national blog and a 2024 agreement PDF officially hosted by the City of Houston
notes: RESOLVED 2026-07-08 (see texas_ohio_legal_followup_source_audit_2026-07-08.md Sec.3-4): corrects the prior session's assumption that Houston non-safety employees likely have no bargaining channel absent confirmed Ch. 146 adoption. The specific recognition-clause department/classification list was not independently confirmed via a direct read of the agreement's own text this session -- the located official PDF did not extract as readable text through this session's tooling; flagged for a future direct read

legal_or_institutional_claim: An official City of Houston HR page hosts the 2024 HOPE meet-and-confer agreement, and a separate official page hosts a 2021 predecessor
source_title_or_description: City of Houston Human Resources, Employee Relations meet-and-confer agreement pages
notes: Located this session; the PDF did not extract as readable text through this session's web-fetch tooling (a tooling limitation, not a document-authenticity concern) -- a future acquisition session should use this project's own ingest/extract_text.py pipeline instead

legal_or_institutional_claim: An official City of Houston HR page hosts a Meet & Confer Agreement for police (HPOU), separate from the fire and non-safety agreements
source_title_or_description: City of Houston Human Resources, Classified Testing meet-and-confer agreement page
notes: Located this session, resolving the prior session's flag that no official Houston police copy had been found; the specific contract-year currency of this URL (vs. a reported 2024-2025 ratified successor) was not independently confirmed

legal_or_institutional_claim: The City of Columbus's fire union, IAFF Local 67, has an official city-hosted CBA (2023-2026) covering ~1,700 uniformed Division of Fire employees, cross-checkable against a SERB-filed 2023 fact-finding report for the same bargaining round
source_title_or_description: City of Columbus Human Resources, Labor Relations page (IAFF CBA); Columbus Firefighters Local 67 'About Us' page; SERB fact-finding report 23-MED-07-0560

legal_or_institutional_claim: The City of Columbus hosts at least two non-safety union CBAs -- AFSCME Local 1632 (2024-2027, and a 2021-2024 predecessor) and CWA (2023-2026) -- both on the same official labor-relations portal as the police/fire CBAs
source_title_or_description: City of Columbus Human Resources, Labor Relations page (AFSCME 1632 and CWA CBAs)
notes: RESOLVED 2026-07-08 (see texas_ohio_legal_followup_source_audit_2026-07-08.md Sec.6): resolves the prior session's 'non-safety contract identity not yet enumerated' flag. Exact occupation-class composition of each union (clerical vs. public-works vs. mixed vs. technical) not yet confirmed via a direct recognition-clause read -- flagged for a future acquisition-planning follow-up

legal_or_institutional_claim: San Antonio operates under full Chapter 174 collective bargaining for police; a 2022-2026 police CBA and a fire CBA are posted on the city's own website
source_title_or_description: City of San Antonio, City Attorney's Office, Collective Bargaining page
notes: Both a 2022-2026 police CBA PDF and a fire CBA PDF (CBA-SAFD.pdf) were directly located on sanantonio.gov this session

legal_or_institutional_claim: San Antonio voters considered (and per secondary reporting, rejected) a May 2021 ballot measure (Proposition B) to repeal Chapter 174 collective-bargaining adoption for the police union
source_title_or_description: Ballotpedia, 'San Antonio, Texas, Proposition B, Repeal Police Collective Bargaining Initiative (May 2021)'
notes: _Repeal_Police_Collective_Bargaining_Initiative_(May_2021)

legal_or_institutional_claim: Austin and Houston use Chapter 142 meet-and-confer (not Chapter 174 collective bargaining) for police and/or fire; Dallas and Fort Worth are also reported to use meet-and-confer
source_title_or_description: Web-search synthesis citing a Texas House bill-analysis document's historical account of meet-and-confer adoption dates by city
notes: Austin's and Houston's meet-and-confer status is independently corroborated by each city's own current labor-relations web pages (see the two municipal_source rows below); Dallas's and Fort Worth's status rests on this secondary bill-analysis source alone and was not independently re-confirmed via a primary Dallas or Fort Worth city source this session

legal_or_institutional_claim: Austin Police Association meet-and-confer agreement (2024-2029) and Austin Firefighters Association Local 975 CBA (2023-2025, with an October 2025 proposed successor) are posted on the city's own labor-relations pages
source_title_or_description: City of Austin, Labor Relations, Police Meet and Confer Agreement / Fire Collective Bargaining Agreement pages
notes: Directly confirmed this session via austintexas.gov's own labor-relations pages, which post current and prior agreement PDFs and negotiation-schedule documents

legal_or_institutional_claim: City of Houston non-safety municipal employees are governed by a civil-service classification and compensation/pay-plan system, not a negotiated collective bargaining agreement ('This guide's contents apply to municipal employees only, and generally not to police officers or firefighters')
source_title_or_description: City of Houston Human Resources, Civil Service & EEO Division / Municipal Employee Guidebook
notes: Directly quoted from the city's own HR guidebook page this session; corroborates the Chapter 617 default-prohibition finding for a specific, named city

legal_or_institutional_claim: A City of Houston Fire Department CBA exists (2024 effective date referenced), but the copy located this session is hosted by a local television station (khou.com), not an official City of Houston page
source_title_or_description: KHOU 11 (Houston), hosted PDF of a City of Houston Fire Department collective bargaining agreement
notes: This is a news-organization-hosted copy, not a primary city source -- an official houstontx.gov labor-relations or Fire Department copy should be located and substituted before any future acquisition, consistent with this project's preference for official/portal sources over local news

legal_or_institutional_claim: Ohio Revised Code Chapter 4117 (Public Employees' Collective Bargaining) is a single statute covering essentially all Ohio public employees statewide -- state, county, and municipal, safety and non-safety alike -- administered by the State Employment Relations Board (SERB)
source_title_or_description: Ohio Revised Code Chapter 4117, general chapter text and Section 4117.01 (Definitions)
notes: Confirmed via the official Ohio Laws site (codes.ohio.gov) and corroborated by Justia's aggregation of the same chapter

legal_or_institutional_claim: ORC 4117.14 requires SERB-supervised mediation, then a three-member SERB fact-finding panel if mediation fails 45 days before contract expiration; fact-finding criteria include comparable-position pay, public welfare, and employer finances
source_title_or_description: Ohio Revised Code Section 4117.14 (Employee organization; certification, mediation, fact-finding, conciliation procedures)
notes: Directly fetched and read this session; corroborated by SERB's own Conciliation Guidebook PDF

legal_or_institutional_claim: Employees named in ORC 4117.14(D)(1) -- police and firefighters, State Highway Patrol members, deputy sheriffs, dispatchers employed by police/fire/sheriff's departments, exclusive nurses' units, employees of state schools for the deaf/blind, State Employees Retirement System employees, and corrections/psychiatric-institution guards -- are prohibited from striking and are routed automatically to binding, final-offer, issue-by-issue conciliation if fact-finding fails
source_title_or_description: Ohio Revised Code Section 4117.14(D)(1) and (G)
notes: This no-strike/conciliation-eligible list is broader than 'police and fire' alone -- directly relevant to this project's own already-developed dispatcher public-safety-adjacent category and should be tracked precisely, not simplified to a police/fire-only boundary, in any future Ohio-specific hypothesis language

legal_or_institutional_claim: Public employees not named on the ORC 4117.14(D)(1) no-strike list (including teachers, clerical, and public-works employees) retain a conditional statutory strike right after a 10-day notice, subject to a full-consecutive-workday requirement and a 10-workday spacing rule from any prior strike
source_title_or_description: Ohio Revised Code Section 4117.14 (strike-notice and spacing provisions)
notes: Directly fetched and read this session; this is the key structural contrast with both Massachusetts (no strike right for anyone) and Texas (no bargaining rights at all for most non-safety employees)

legal_or_institutional_claim: SERB maintains a single, centralized, publicly searchable online archive of filed collective bargaining agreements, fact-finding reports, and conciliation awards for Ohio public employers since 2012
source_title_or_description: State Employment Relations Board (SERB), View Document Archive
notes: Confirmed directly this session via multiple located example PDFs (a Columbus-area FOP contract, a City of Alliance police conciliation award, a 2023 fact-finding report); a genuinely useful centralized-records resource this project's Massachusetts work does not have an equivalent of

legal_or_institutional_claim: The City of Columbus's own labor-relations webpage hosts a current (2023-2026) Fraternal Order of Police Capital City Lodge No. 9 collective bargaining agreement, and reportedly hosts all six of the city's union contracts in total
source_title_or_description: City of Columbus, Human Resources, Labor Relations page
notes: The police CBA PDF itself was directly located and confirmed (fop-cba-2023-2026.pdf); the claim that 'all six' city contracts are hosted on this page, and the specific identity of the other five (including any non-safety unit), was not independently enumerated this session and should be confirmed with a direct page visit before acquisition

legal_or_institutional_claim: The City of Cincinnati's labor-relations webpage hosts multiple current (2025-2028) union contracts, including at least one clearly non-safety unit (CODE -- Cincinnati Organized and Dedicated Employees) and a Teamsters contract
source_title_or_description: City of Cincinnati, Human Resources, Labor Relations page
notes: Both the CODE and Teamsters contract pages were directly located this session; the exact occupation-class composition of each bargaining unit (clerical vs. public-works vs. mixed) was not read in depth this session and needs confirmation before classification under this project's controlled vocabulary

legal_or_institutional_claim: The City of Cleveland maintains a dedicated 'Collective Bargaining Agreements' page on its official Human Resources site
source_title_or_description: City of Cleveland, Human Resources, Collective Bargaining Agreements page
